news.title: Referee Anthony Taylor retires from football after World Cup appearance
news.lede: 47-year-old took charge of two tournament matches. Taylor has refereed in the Premier League for 16 years. Anthony Taylor has announced his retirement from football with immediate effect after officiating in his second World Cup. The 47-year-old took charge of two matches in the United States, Spain’s last-16 win over Portugal and a group game between Senegal and Iraq, but was not given any games in the final stages due to England’s progression to the semi-finals.

news.title: Here are 48 reasons to cherish the World Cup (minus the Geopolitics)
news.lede: The Geopolitics World Cup is already fading from view, replaced by a thin July gruel of pre-season friendlies and transfer tales. But there’s still time to cast another look back over the 48-team, 104-game, 308-goal leviathan that has dominated the last six weeks of our lives. Without further ado, here are 48 reasons to cherish this year’s World Cup (minus the Geopolitics). The very first game between Mexico and South Africa saw three red cards dished out, ITV showing off their studio’s Manhattan backdrop, and a duck anointed as a national hero.

news.title: Tebas goes all in: "FIFA is destroying football, Infantino must go"
news.lede: Javier Tebas returned to Madrid from New York after watching the second World Cup of the Spanish national team. The president of LaLiga returns happy with the victory but very angry with the role of FIFA throughout the tournament. '"FIFA organizes things as it pleases, for its own interests, not for football. So if they need a 27-minute break, they do it.

news.title: Nico Williams, Ferran Torres and Spain’s adaptable World Cup excellence | Jeff Rueter
news.lede: The two substitutes combined on the World Cup winning goal not by changing the game, but by offering it what it needed, when it was needed. As Spain built into the second half of extra-time of Sunday’s World Cup final, it was already clear that Argentina hoped to make them suffer. Every challenge left no stud unshown. Their defenders labored to block striker Mikel Oyarzabal from his typical lanes of service, with Spain taking 11 of their 20 attempts from beyond the box.

news.title: World Cup becoming a 64-team tournament would create logistical challenges - so can Gianni Infantino's idea work?
news.lede: This year's World Cup is finally over - and after 48 teams took part, it was a long, long tournament. But just as we bid to say goodbye to the 2026 edition, this competition may be getting even bigger. FIFA president Gianni Infantino says any further expansion of the World Cup for 2030 - where six countries are set to host the tournament - ‌will be discussed now the 2026 World Cup has finished. And one solution FIFA are discussing is a 64-team tournament.

news.title: Chelsea’s good World Cup omens | News | Official Site
news.lede: The end of a World Cup is usually a good sign for Chelsea, with some of the most successful and important seasons in our history coming immediately after the international showpiece. The latest edition of the FIFA World Cup came to a close on Sunday evening, when Spain were crowned champions for the second time with a 1-0 win over Argentina in the 2026 final in New Jersey. People sometimes talk about a World Cup hangover, with some of the players who star at the tournament having difficult seasons afterwards as their summer exertions and late return for pre-season catch up with them. However, if history is anything to go by, that is unlikely to be the case for the 11 Chelsea players who competed at the 2026 World Cup, with the post-World Cup seasons producing some of the Blues’ most memorable campaigns.

news.title: The US should never host another tournament after World Cup debacle
news.lede: The moral, financial and logistical issues that plagued this World Cup should be enough to disqualify the US from hosting major football tournaments until serious changes are made, argues Richard Jolly. From reproductive rights to climate change to Big Tech, The Independent is on the ground when the story is developing. Whether it's investigating the financials of Elon Musk's pro-Trump PAC or producing our latest documentary, 'The A Word', which shines a light on the American women fighting for reproductive rights, we know how important it is to parse out the facts from the messaging. At such a critical moment in US history, we need reporters on the ground.

news.title: Argentina dreams of another Messi for the 2030 World Cup: the fact about his son Thiago that has attracted attention
news.lede: In the world of football, Leo Messi's immediate future is generating countless questions. However, among the sea of Argentine fans, a mathematical detail that until recently went unnoticed has gained traction, fueling the fans' greatest hopes. Popular attention has focused on the year 2030, the centenary of the World Cup. By then, Thiago Messi, the eldest son of the Rosario-born star, will have reached the age of majority, turning 18 in November.
